articles.title: Nightjars: Birds Making a Big Comeback
articles.summary: Nightjars are special birds that come out at dusk and dawn. They are making a big comeback in a park called the South Downs National Park. Last year, 78 nightjars were counted, which is double from five years ago. People are helping by taking care of the park's habitat, like keeping dogs on leads and staying on paths. Nightjars fly a long way from Africa each spring and nest on the ground. They have a funny nickname, 'goatsucker,' from an old story that they drank goat milk, but that's not true. The birds are important and need protection because their numbers dropped in the past. Now, with better care, they are doing well again.

articles.title: What Happens When Sourdough Bread Rises?
articles.summary: Sourdough bread is a special type of bread that many people love for its taste and health benefits. Scientists wanted to learn more about how it is made. They studied tiny living things called microbes, like bacteria and yeast, that help the dough rise. These microbes eat parts of the wheat flour, especially fibers called arabinoxylans. The scientists found that during fermentation, which is when the dough rises, some fibers change from a good type to a less good type. This can affect how the bread feels and tastes. They also discovered that enzymes in the wheat, not just the microbes, play a big role in this change. The research helps us understand why sourdough bread is unique and might make it even better in the future.

articles.title: The Smell of Mummies Tells Ancient Stories
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered that the old smell of Egyptian mummies can teach us about how people were preserved long ago. Mummies are bodies wrapped in cloth from ancient Egypt, and they have a special musty scent. Researchers at the University of Bristol found that this smell comes from chemicals used during mummification, not just from decay. By studying tiny pieces of mummies, they identified smells from fats, oils, beeswax, plant resins, and bitumen. These chemicals show that mummification methods changed over 2,000 years, becoming more advanced with expensive materials. The study helps us learn about Egyptian history and how they cared for the dead.

articles.title: Scientists Find a New Kind of Quantum State with Frustrated Atoms
articles.summary: Scientists at UC Santa Barbara are studying a strange new state of matter called a quantum state that happens when atoms get "frustrated." They use materials with tiny magnets at the atomic level. In some shapes, like triangles, these magnets can't line up properly because they want to point opposite to each other but can't all do that at once. This makes them frustrated and creates unusual magnetic states. The researchers also look at another type of frustration with electrons sharing bonds. They found a rare material where both types of frustration exist together. This discovery could help in making future quantum technologies, like better computers, but the scientists say it's mostly about learning basic science for now. They hope this work will lead to new ways to control these frustrated systems.

articles.title: Turning Sunlight into Fuel with New Materials
articles.summary: Scientists have found a new way to use sunlight to make fuel, which is like turning light into energy we can use. They are studying special materials called polyheptazine imides that can absorb sunlight and help create chemical reactions. These materials are made from carbon and nitrogen and are safe, cheap, and stable. By using computer models, researchers can predict how these materials will work without making every one in a lab. They tested 53 different metal ions to see which ones make the materials better at separating charges, which is important for making fuel. This discovery could help speed up research and lead to new ways to produce clean energy from the sun.

articles.title: Farmers Face High Costs for Growing Food
articles.summary: Farmers in the UK are having a hard time because the prices of fertiliser and fuel are going up a lot. Fertiliser helps plants grow, and fuel is used for farm machines. A farmer named Andrew Williamson says it is very expensive now, and this makes it tough for farmers to make enough money. He grows crops like wheat and barley on his farm. The high costs come after two bad harvests, which means farmers already had less food to sell. Livestock farmers, who raise animals, are also affected because they buy fertiliser when they need it, not ahead of time. This situation makes farming risky because farmers have to wait a long time to see if their crops will sell well. The article explains why this is a big problem for farmers and how it might make food more expensive for everyone.
